source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
CONTACT Sarah Jakob           sj@jakob-uhg.de
1
  The terms China, Peoples Republic of China (PRC) and Chinese Communist Party (CCP) are used to
  describe the current government of China under General Secretary Xi Jinping. ‘China’ is not intended
  to refer to the country itself with the entirety of its peoples and cultures.

                                                            TRANSNATIONAL LEGAL THEORY                   295

allow the country to inﬂuence ‘the construction and ﬂow of legal norms’2
outside its legislative jurisdiction. The world economy is increasingly dependent
on the Chinese market and corporations seek the potential proﬁts that can be
made in this market.3 Via the market, corporations are increasingly aﬀected by
the Chinese political system and geopolitical tensions.4 This is similar, yet not
identical, to the asymmetry of power which has been working to the beneﬁt of
the Northern American and European states—and their ability to use law for
the consolidation of this power imbalance.5 The importance of their markets,
military power and these countries’ experience and resources enable them to
inﬂuence, for example, policy prescriptions of international institutions, concepts
of law or the hierarchy within the law and to advance national law,6 in order to
secure the interests of their market actors via the transnational eﬀects of law.7 The
‘transnational’, as Claire Cutler perceives it, serves the interests of global capital-
ism. It is a ‘political project’ which strengthens investor rights against states and
keeps corporate responsibility in the realm of soft law.8
    This paper argues that the ‘transnational’ is a tool that is not bound to the
aim of strengthening investor rights and global capitalism but can also serve
particular state interests by using global capitalism while not necessarily
prioritising capitalist demands and rationale. In the case of China, the
CCP might be able to gain inﬂuence by establishing new internal modes of
regulation.9 Since 2014,10 the CCP is in the process of creating a social
